desc: All town streetlights converted to dark sky rated LED protecting bird migration, reducing energy cost and carbon footprint by 280 metric tons.
more_text: Swampscott's 1414 streetlights received an energy-efficient LED upgrade thanks in large part to a $225,000 state grant from the Department of Energy Resources Green Communities program. Reducing energy costs enabled the town to reinvest its financial savings in schools, police, fire, municipal infrastructure and other initiatives. More importantly, the reduction in energy consumption yielded an equivalent reduction in CO2 emissions. Together with 76 other municipalities, this statewide program achieved an annual reduction in GHG emission of more than 10,000 metric tons.

desc: First microforest on the North Shore begins increasing biodiversity, cooling and filtering the air, and storing carbon.
more_text:
Microforests are dense, tiny forests with high biodiversity. The technique is a form of afforestation, or the act of planting a forest where there wasn't one before. This is different than reforestation, which restores an existing forest. The challenge of both forms of planting is the method. When a single tree species is planted over hectares of land, it creates a biological desert. These sites become mono-crop fields with few insects, animals, or other plants.

Forests need multiple layers of plant and animal life to thrive. These layers create a web of biodiversity between fungi, animals, trees, and everything in between. Each layer helps the others become healthier. Traditional forests are home to an estimated 80% of all Earth's land-based species. Tiny forests aim to replicate that level of biodiversity on a small scale.

desc: Town vote preserved these 5 acres of green space and tree canopy, providing climate cooling and carbon sequestration, and boosting biodiversity.
more_text: Swampscott's Open Space and Recreation Plan takes climate change, higher water levels, more intense storms, etc. into account in planning. Green spaces are essential to counteracting the effects of climate change. Trees and other vegetation in green spaces mitigate heat by cooling neighborhoods through shade and evapotranspiration, a critical tool against the urban heat island effect. Plants sequester carbon by removing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere through photosynthesis while also improving air quality by filtering pollutants. Green spaces, including parks and rain gardens, also help absorb and manage stormwater, reducing flooding and filtering water naturally.
note:
Town Meeting https://patch.com/massachusetts/swampscott/swampscotts-hawthorne-property-purchase-sails-through-town-meeting

desc: Elementary school opens with geothermal heating and cooling, and LEED Gold Certification.
more_text:
LEED is a program that provides third-party verification of green buildings. Looking at building projects holistically, the goals of the LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) initiative are to improve the DNA of buildings to support healthy, safe living in our environmental habitats and human communities. LEED also acknowledges the huge risks presented by climate change, so it gives priority to both greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions reductions as well as the embodied carbon of buildings. Of the many aspects considered for LEED certification, the largest qualifying category is reducing the contribution of buildings to climate change.

Geothermal heat pumps (GHPs), take advantage of the constant temperature of the shallow earth (40°-70°F/4.5°-21°C) to efficiently exchange temperatures, heating buildings in the winter and cooling them in the summer. This ground temperature is warmer than the air above it during the winter and cooler than the air in the summer. The GHP takes advantage of the constant subsurface temperatures by exchanging heat with the earth through a ground heat exchanger. Compared to air-source systems, geothermal systems have been shown to be quieter, last longer, and require less maintenance, and they do not depend on the temperature of the outside air. Geothermal systems are typically more expensive than air-source systems, but the additional costs are often returned with energy savings.
note:
U.S. Green Building Council

U.S. Dept of Energy https://www.energy.gov/energysaver/geothermal-heat-pumps

desc: First EV charging station celebrates 10th anniversary with expansion to 10 sites accommodating 50 vehicles throughout town.
more_text:
Transportation was a major source of carbon emissions in Swampscott, accounting for 36% of greenhouse gas emissions compared to 28% nationwide. In the first quarter of the 21st century emissions from the transportation sector nationally were increasing in absolute terms more than any other sector. A major obstacle to EV ownership was the small number of charging stations compared to the ubiquity of gas stations, so increasing the availability of charging stations was crucial to motivate the transition to electric vehicles.

Transportation has been a major source of carbon emissions, accounting for 28% of greenhouse gases nationwide. In Swampscott the proportion was even higher, at 36%. In the first quarter of the 21st century emissions from the transportation sector nationally were increasing in absolute terms more than any other sector. A major obstacle to EV ownership was the small number of charging stations compared to the ubiquity of gas stations, so increasing the availability of charging stations was crucial to motivate the transition to electric vehicles.

desc: Responding to escalating threat of higher flood waters, Fish House relocated to preserve its historical value.
more_text:
Evidence of the town's historic marine-based industry, the Fish House was built by the Town in 1896 and is the oldest, active municipal facility of its kind in the U.S. Designed by Lynn architect Henry W. Rogers, the Fish House is listed in the National Register of Historic Places along with many of his other local works. The Swampscott Fish House was built to offer fisherman leasable units in a single building, which replaced the numerous small fishing shanties previously dotting the Swampscott coastline. The building is still used by fishermen, making it the only municipal fish house in continuous operation on the East Coast.

Coastal storm and ocean inundation has increased rapidly. Historically, superstorms or hurricanes occurred about once every decade or two. In the 21st century super-storm events have been occurring at a rate of approximately two major storms per year, with significant damage storms occurring at a frequency of three to four times per year. The accumulated damage from these storms is extensive. Nowhere is this issue more evident than along the Fisherman's Beach. Here, successive storms have severely damaged infrastructure, including the historic and iconic Fisherman's Beach Fish House as well as the Town Pier, and the lower downtown areas of the Swampscott business district. Here the topography is low and coastal storm energy is translated onto the built environment even during weak-moderate storm events; and during extreme events, the area succumbs to damage that is both dramatic and costly.

Storm surge is an abnormal rise of water generated by a storm, over and above the predicted astronomical tide. The continual rise in sea level has provided a higher base on which storm surge accumulates and tropical storms became slower-moving, which resulted in rain, wind, and storm surge persisting over multiple tidal cycles, thus causing more damage. In addition to tropical storms and hurricanes, nor'easters are intense storms that develop from low pressure centers that derive their energy from the strong temperature gradients that commonly occur just off the U.S. East Coast during winter. They move along the coast of Massachusetts with wind blowing across the Atlantic Ocean from the northeast, directly onto the shore.

desc: Annual Thanksgiving Day game played at Phillips Park for last time as football field yields to encroaching marsh.
more_text: Accelerated rates of global, or eustatic, sea level change are driven principally by increases in the volume of the ocean from two primary factors: thermal expansion and melting ice sheets. Steady increases in global atmospheric temperature served to expand sea water molecules, which increased ocean volume. Increased global temperatures also resulted in the melting of glaciers and continental ice masses—such as the Greenland ice sheet that covered terrestrial areas, not ice‐covered ocean as in the Arctic—which contributed significant amounts of freshwater input to the Earth's oceans. From 2020 to 2050 sea level along the East Coast rose 14 inches.
